name: The Delta Blues Room
description: A dimly lit, smoke-filled joint on Bourbon Street that caters to the Black community with a mix of blues and jazz performers.

name: Sugar's Sweet Spot
description: This cozy bar in the predominantly white Uptown area offers live jazz music nightly, drawing a mixed crowd but remaining largely white patronage.

name: The Cotton Club Cabaret
description: An upscale venue known for its racially segregated showrooms, with Black performers entertaining primarily white clientele upstairs and a smaller Black audience downstairs.

name: Whispers of the Crescent
description: A secretive jazz club hidden down a narrow alley in the French Quarter, favored by both local artists and curious tourists who can afford it.

name: Blue Moon Bar
description: Located on Chartres Street, this modest bar is popular among Black workers after their shifts end, offering cheap drinks and occasional live music.

name: The Silver Serenaders Club
description: A posh establishment in the heart of Storyville that was once notorious for its jazz scene but now caters to a white elite looking for nostalgic entertainment.

name: Harlem Hot Spot
description: An underground jazz club known for defying racial barriers, attracting both Black and white artists and an eclectic mix of patrons late into the night.

name: Bayou Blues Bar
description: A rustic bar in the Seventh Ward where local musicians gather to play traditional blues tunes, surrounded by old wooden décor and flickering lantern light.

name: The Midnight Lounge
description: An elegant speakeasy near Canal Street that operates under a veil of secrecy; entry is restricted to members only, with jazz performances behind closed doors.

name: Riverside Rhythms
description: A riverside bar in Algiers Point where musicians perform while patrons enjoy the Mississippi River views and drinks under twinkling strings of lights.

name: The Velvet Veil
description: A hidden speakeasy where jazz flows as freely as the illegal liquor, known for its exclusive clientele and dimly lit corners.

name: Ruby's Red Carpet Club
description: An upscale brothel catering to white businessmen and politicians, offering discreet services in lavish rooms.

name: The Golden Dice Casino
description: A notorious gambling den run by the city's underworld figures, featuring rigged games and secret backrooms.

name: Mama Celeste's Paradise
description: A popular Black-owned speakeasy in the heart of Tremé, famous for its live jazz performances and friendly atmosphere.

name: The Midnight Flirtation Club
description: A mixed-race brothel with a strict dress code, offering luxurious accommodations and discreet services.

name: Snake Eyes Saloon
description: A rough-and-tumble gambling den on the edge of Storyville, where card cheats and brawls are commonplace.

name: Sweet Pea's Tea Room
description: An elegant brothel catering to white patrons with a sense of style and class, hidden behind an innocent facade.

name: The Crescent Moon Club
description: A mixed-race speakeasy known for its innovative jazz bands and underground gambling tables.

name: Black Cat Bingo Hall
description: An African American-run casino that doubles as a front for illegal activities, with a strict no-white policy.

name: The Whispering Willow Inn
description: A high-class brothel in the Garden District, favored by wealthy socialites and politicians, offering discreet and luxurious services.

name: Bourbon Street Wharf
description: A bustling dock area teeming with cargo ships and smugglers, notorious for late-night activities under the dim glow of oil lanterns.

name: Delta Trading Co. Warehouse
description: A vast warehouse filled with crates from all over the world, used by the corrupt Delta Trading Company to hide their illicit goods and shady dealings.

name: The Cotton Club Office
description: An office building run by a powerful white-only club where jazz legends play to the rich elite, but rumors of illegal gambling persist behind closed doors.

name: St. Charles Warehouse District
description: A sprawling complex of warehouses owned and operated by various crime syndicates, each with its own secrets hidden within.

name: Treme Dockside Diner
description: A mixed-race diner known for serving up hearty meals to dock workers and sailors, where news of the city flows like water.

name: NOLA Import & Export Office
description: An office run by a white businessman with ties to both legal trade and black market dealings, often frequented by shady characters after dark.

name: The Crescent City Dockworkers Union Hall
description: A union hall that serves as a haven for Black dock workers, offering them protection against exploitation and violence in the dangerous world of shipping.

name: Esplanade Warehouse
description: An old warehouse near the riverfront, once bustling with trade but now mainly used for storing contraband and illegal goods under cover of darkness.

name: French Market Pier
description: A pier lined with small fishing boats and food stalls where vendors sell fresh catch to the market, often serving as a meeting place for smugglers and criminals.

name: Harlem House of Prayer
description: A predominantly Black church that doubles as a sanctuary and safe house for those escaping violence or injustice in the city's underworld.

name: The Crescent City Union Hall
description: A bustling center for the city's labor unions, particularly active in organizing dock workers and musicians.

name: Le Journal de la Nouvelle-Orléans
description: A predominantly French-language newspaper office that also serves as a meeting place for local politicians and activists.

name: The Black Pearl Club
description: A vibrant jazz club owned by African American entrepreneurs, featuring performances by some of the city's most talented musicians.

name: City Hall Annex
description: An administrative building that houses various city departments and is often a hub for political deals and backroom meetings.

name: The Brassworks Union
description: A union hall dedicated to the interests of metal workers, featuring prominent African American leadership in an otherwise white-dominated industry.

name: Courier de Nouvelle-Orléans
description: An English-language newspaper office known for its investigative reporting on local politics and corruption.

name: The Cotton Club
description: A mixed-race establishment that serves as a nightclub during the evenings and a political meeting place during daytime hours.

name: Delta Democratic Headquarters
description: The primary office of the powerful Delta Democratic Party, often visited by both black and white politicians seeking support for their agendas.

name: Mechanics Union Hall
description: A union hall primarily serving the interests of mechanics and auto workers in a predominantly African American neighborhood.

name: The Evening Star Press
description: An office building that houses several small, independent newspapers covering various aspects of city life from different perspectives.

name: Saint Mary's Cathedral
description: A grand but weather-beaten cathedral on Bourbon Street, Saint Mary's is a stark contrast to the jazz clubs and brothels nearby, serving as both a place of worship and refuge for those seeking solace in Noirleans.

name: The Cotton Club
description: An upscale nightclub with strict segregation policies, The Cotton Club is known for its live jazz bands and glamorous clientele. Its basement is rumored to house a speakeasy frequented by the city's elite.

name: Le Chat Noir
description: A French Quarter hotel with an air of mystery, Le Chat Noir caters to artists and musicians drawn to Noirleans' vibrant nightlife. Its rooms are often filled with smoke and whispers of illicit affairs.

name: The Delta Blues Café
description: A soulful restaurant in the Tremé neighborhood, The Delta Blues Café serves up hearty Creole dishes alongside live blues music by local artists. Its clientele is a mix of Black locals and white tourists.

name: Holy Trinity Baptist Church
description: A bustling church in the heart of the Seventh Ward, Holy Trinity Baptist Church serves as a hub for community gatherings and political discussions among the Black population. Its pastor is often consulted by parishioners with troubles.

name: The Grand Hotel Orleans
description: An opulent hotel near Jackson Square, The Grand Hotel Orleans hosts visiting dignitaries and wealthy tourists. Rumor has it that its penthouse suites are the favored hideaways of corrupt officials.

name: The Bourbon Street Brasserie
description: A busy French restaurant in the heart of the Quarter, The Bourbon Street Brasserie offers a blend of traditional French cuisine and Creole flavors. Its patrons include both locals and out-of-town visitors.

name: St. John's Episcopal Church
description: A historic white church located in the Garden District, St. John's Episcopal Church is renowned for its elaborate stained-glass windows and grand architecture. It's also a meeting point for the city's upper class.

name: The Crescent Hotel
description: A stately hotel on Canal Street that offers luxurious accommodations to both business travelers and vacationers. The hotel has a secretive side, with rumors of illegal gambling in its private rooms.

name: Creole Kitchen
description: A cozy restaurant in the Ninth Ward serving classic Creole dishes like gumbo and jambalaya. It's popular among both locals and tourists looking for authentic flavors, often buzzing with lively conversations.

name: Ruby's Apothecary
description: A small, dimly lit pharmacy tucked away in the heart of Treme, known for its potent tonics and secret remedies.

name: The Midnight Diner
description: An all-night diner frequented by night workers and shady characters, serving up greasy spoon fare with a side of gossip.

name: Belle's Super Market
description: A bustling market in the predominantly Black neighborhood, offering fresh produce and rumors under the watchful eye of Belle herself.

name: Whitewash Pharmacy
description: An upscale white-only establishment with a strict demeanor, known for its high-end pharmaceuticals and discreet clientele.

name: Sunrise Cafeteria
description: A popular mixed-race diner near the French Quarter where jazz musicians grab quick bites before their late-night gigs.

name: The Crescent Market
description: A vibrant market in a racially diverse neighborhood, offering a mix of groceries and illicit goods.

name: Soulful Herbs Pharmacy
description: A small but popular pharmacy near Congo Square that sells both traditional medicines and spiritual supplies.

name: Daybreak Diner
description: An early-morning diner where cops and dockworkers meet for breakfast, often exchanging tips on the latest crimes in town.

name: Greenleaf Market
description: A quiet market in a predominantly white area that caters to upper-class residents with high-quality produce and imported goods.

name: Mama's Night Owl Pharmacy
description: An unusual pharmacy open until the early hours, run by an elderly woman who deals in both legal medications and less savory substances.

name: The Rusty Coin
description: A dimly lit pawn shop in the heart of the French Quarter, known for its shady dealings and hidden treasures.

name: Mourning Glory Funeral Home
description: Located on a quiet street in Treme, this funeral home is often bustling with mourners and the occasional mysterious figure.

name: Pillars of Zion Tenement
description: A towering tenement building housing many Black families who have lived through the struggles of 1930s New Orleans, each floor whispering tales of hardship and hope.

name: Whispering Willows Funeral Parlor
description: Situated in a predominantly white neighborhood, this funeral parlor is known for its somber yet regal atmosphere.

name: The Last Chance Pawn & Jewelry
description: A small pawn shop in the Ninth Ward with a clientele that includes both locals and out-of-towners looking to turn their troubles into cash.

name: Oakwood Tenement Complex
description: An old, mixed-race tenement complex on the edge of the city where racial tensions often simmer beneath the surface.

name: Elysium Memorial Parlor
description: A grand and modern funeral parlor in Uptown that caters to a more affluent clientele, both Black and white.

name: Silver Linings Pawn Shop
description: Known for its eccentric owner who collects everything from antique guns to rare records, this pawn shop is a favorite among collectors.

name: Harbor View Tenement
description: A dilapidated tenement near the port where sailors and dock workers often find cheap lodging after nights of revelry and gambling.

name: St. Augustine's Funeral Services
description: Located in Algiers, this funeral home is a pillar of the Black community known for its traditional services and vibrant music at wakes.

name: The Blue Piano Club
description: A dimly lit, jazz-infused club in the heart of the Black district, where musicians and patrons alike lose themselves in the rhythm.

name: Precinct 3 Police Station
description: Located near Congo Square, this station is notorious for its corrupt officers and unofficial segregation policies.

name: River's End Ferry Terminal
description: A bustling transport hub where sailors, smugglers, and detectives converge on the banks of the Mississippi River.

name: Whispering Willow Church
description: An old church with a reputation for hosting secret meetings between local politicians and bootleggers.

name: The Cotton Seed Hotel
description: A rundown establishment catering to transient laborers, known for its dubious clientele and cheap rooms.

name: Papa Joe's Fish Market
description: A crowded market filled with the aroma of fresh fish and lively haggling between vendors and buyers.

name: Crescent City Wharf
description: A large dock area where goods are unloaded from ships, often under suspicious circumstances involving local gangs.

name: The Silver Dollar Casino
description: An upscale gambling den frequented by white elites and politicians, strictly enforcing a whites-only policy.

name: St. Louis Street Station
description: A major train station connecting Noirleans to other cities, where passengers come and go amidst the city's political intrigue.

name: Bayou House of Cards
description: An illegal card game parlor hidden in a bayou shantytown, attracting gamblers from all walks of life seeking quick profits.

name: The Bronze Key
description: A swanky nightclub where the city's jazz elite and underground movers meet, often shrouded in secrecy.

name: Baldwin's Barber Emporium
description: Renowned for its impeccable service and tight-knit community of regulars, Baldwin’s is a meeting place for neighborhood gossip and political discussion.

name: The Holy Trinity Missionary Baptist Church
description: A spiritual sanctuary for the Black community, it's also a hub for social advocacy and support networks during tough times.

name: Euphemia's Funeral Parlor
description: Famous among locals for its unique blend of traditional mourning rituals and lively jazz funerals celebrating life’s joys.

name: The Blue Moon Cafe
description: A cozy cafe that doubles as a community center, serving up delicious soul food alongside the latest local news.

name: Mystic Temple of Honor
description: An exclusive social club and burial society providing funerary aid to members, often bustling with political intrigue.

name: Lincoln Park Baptist Church
description: A grand church that serves as both a religious sanctuary and a center for civil rights activities.

name: The Corner Clip Shop
description: A bustling barbershop that's also the nerve center of community news, from job opportunities to police raids.

name: St. Augustine's Funeral Home & Chapel
description: Respected for its traditional and modern funeral services, it’s a place where all walks of life come together in times of mourning.

name: The Golden Note Music Hall
description: A night club dedicated to jazz, swing, and blues, known for its underground gambling scenes and illicit deals.

name: The Juke Joint
description: A cramped, dimly lit bar on Basin Street where jazz flows and cards are dealt under the watchful eye of a mixed-race clientele.

name: Whispers Down by the Docks
description: A dilapidated dockside tavern frequented by sailors and local gangsters, known for its rough trade and late-night revelry.

name: The Blue Moon Brothel
description: An upscale brothel on Bourbon Street catering to white clientele, with strict racial segregation enforced at the door.

name: Lucky's Last Chance Hotel
description: A run-down flophouse near the riverfront where outcasts and vagrants huddle together for warmth and respite.

name: Voodoo Lady Saloon
description: A mysterious bar in the Tremé neighborhood, known for its mixed-race patrons who mingle with a backdrop of voodoo artifacts and jazz music.

name: The Cotton Club Casino
description: An illegal gambling den located in a warehouse near the waterfront, attracting both Black and white gamblers looking for action after dark.

name: The Golden Dollar Nightclub
description: A swanky club in the heart of Storyville where jazz greats play to an exclusively white crowd indulging in drinks and vice.

name: Rusty's Rowdy Room
description: An all-night bar on Canal Street with a clientele that ranges from dock workers to bootleggers, where trouble often brews over card games.

name: The Red Light Inn
description: A brothel located in the heart of Congo Square that caters primarily to Black clients, blending business with cultural significance.

name: Mama Roux's Tea Room
description: An intimate cafe on Esplanade Avenue serving as a meeting ground for local political figures and underworld bosses.
